content:
Here’s a quick way to see why 17 is the first prime that fails to divide

S = 9 + 9² + ⋯ + 9²⁰¹⁰.

1.  Rewrite the sum in closed‐form:
   S = 9(9²⁰¹⁰ − 1)/(9 − 1) = (9²⁰¹¹ − 9)/8.

2.  For p=2,3,5,7,11,13 one checks that the sum of the relevant powers of 9 mod p is 0, so each of these divides S.

3.  For p=17:
   • 9 mod 17 has order 8, with cycle of powers
     9,13,15,16, 8, 4, 2, 1 (sum = 68 ≡ 0 mod 17).
   • 2010 ≡ 2 (mod 8), so only the first two terms 9+13 ≡ 22 ≡ 5 (mod 17) survive.
   Hence S ≢ 0 (mod 17).

Thus the smallest prime not dividing S is

\boxed{17}.

message.content:
Here’s a quick way to see why 17 is the first prime that fails to divide

S = 9 + 9² + ⋯ + 9²⁰¹⁰.

1.  Rewrite the sum in closed‐form:
   S = 9(9²⁰¹⁰ − 1)/(9 − 1) = (9²⁰¹¹ − 9)/8.

2.  For p=2,3,5,7,11,13 one checks that the sum of the relevant powers of 9 mod p is 0, so each of these divides S.

3.  For p=17:
   • 9 mod 17 has order 8, with cycle of powers
     9,13,15,16, 8, 4, 2, 1 (sum = 68 ≡ 0 mod 17).
   • 2010 ≡ 2 (mod 8), so only the first two terms 9+13 ≡ 22 ≡ 5 (mod 17) survive.
   Hence S ≢ 0 (mod 17).

Thus the smallest prime not dividing S is

\boxed{17}.
